title: Telehealth interventions for managing chronic pain: a systematic review
authors: Chen Y, Zhang L, Wang H
abstract: OBJECTIVE: To evaluate telehealth interventions for chronic pain management. METHODS: We searched PubMed, EMBASE, and Cochrane for RCTs comparing telehealth to usual care for chronic pain in adults. 23 RCTs met inclusion criteria (N=4521). RESULTS: Telehealth interventions showed moderate effect on pain reduction (SMD -0.42, 95% CI -0.58 to -0.26, p<0.001). Patient satisfaction was high (82%). Attrition rates averaged 18%. Subgroup analysis showed stronger effects for cognitive-behavioral approaches (SMD -0.61) vs educational interventions (SMD -0.22). CONCLUSIONS: Telehealth is effective for chronic pain, particularly CBT-based programs.

title: Mobile health applications for low back pain self-management
abstract: PURPOSE: To assess the feasibility and effectiveness of a mobile health app for low back pain. METHODS: Prospective cohort study of 156 adults with chronic low back pain using the app for 12 weeks. RESULTS: Pain scores decreased from baseline (VAS 6.2 to 3.8, p<0.01). 72% of participants reported improved function. App adherence was 65% at 12 weeks. No adverse events were reported. CONCLUSIONS: Mobile health apps show promise for low back pain self-management.

title: Virtual reality for acute pain management in burn patients: a randomized controlled trial
authors: Thompson A, Lee B, Garcia C
abstract: BACKGROUND: Virtual reality (VR) may reduce pain during wound care procedures. METHODS: Double-blind RCT of 80 adult burn patients randomized to VR (n=40) or standard care (n=40) during dressing changes. Primary outcome: pain intensity (NRS 0-10). RESULTS: VR group reported significantly lower pain (NRS 3.2 vs 6.7, p<0.001, Cohen's d=1.4). Anxiety was also reduced (STAI 32 vs 48, p<0.001). Procedure time was similar between groups (p=0.42). No VR-related adverse effects. CONCLUSIONS: VR is highly effective for procedural pain in burn patients.
